content:
# TOTAL DEPRAVITY: A COMPLETE STUDY GUIDE
### Iron & Ink Reformed Theological Studies

---

## PREFACE: WHAT IS AT STAKE

Total Depravity is not a peripheral doctrine. It is the foundation upon which the entire structure of redemptive theology rests. Get this wrong, and you will misunderstand sin, misunderstand grace, misunderstand the cross, misunderstand conversion, and — most critically — misunderstand God. The acronym TULIP does not begin with Total Depravity by accident. Every subsequent doctrine of grace depends on an accurate reckoning with what man is apart from God.

This guide will not soften the doctrine to make it palatable. It will exegete it, defend it, situate it confessionally, and arm you to answer the objections raised against it.

---

## PART ONE: DEFINITION AND CLARIFICATION

### What Total Depravity Is

Total Depravity is the biblical teaching that the Fall of Adam in Genesis 3 has so thoroughly corrupted human nature that every faculty of man — intellect, will, affections, conscience, and body — is polluted by sin and bent away from God. The corruption is **total in its extent**, meaning no part of man escapes the contamination of the Fall.

This doctrine holds four essential claims:

1. **Every part of man is corrupted.** Sin is not confined to one compartment of human nature. It has penetrated the mind, the will, the emotions, and the moral sense.
2. **Man is by nature spiritually dead.** Apart from regenerating grace, man does not merely need assistance — he is without spiritual life entirely.
3. **Man is by nature unable to choose God.** The unregenerate will is not weakened or injured — it is in bondage. It cannot and will not choose God savingly apart from divine intervention.
4. **Man is by nature under God's just wrath.** This is not merely a condition — it is a verdict. Man stands condemned before God from the point of conception.

### What Total Depravity Is Not

Precision here is not optional. Common misunderstandings must be named and rejected:

- **It does not mean every person is as sinful as they could possibly be.** Total Depravity does not teach that every unregenerate person acts with maximum wickedness at every moment. God's common grace restrains sin. Unregenerate people perform acts of civic virtue, demonstrate kindness, and maintain moral standards. Total Depravity does not deny this.

- **It does not mean man has no rational capacity.** Fallen man retains the image of God (*imago Dei*) in a formal sense — he can reason, create, and govern. What he cannot do is deploy these faculties rightly *toward God* in a saving sense.

- **It does not mean man feels no moral tension.** Romans 2:14-15 tells us the unregenerate have the work of the law written on their hearts. They have conscience. What they do not have is the ability to respond savingly to what their conscience reveals.

- **The more precise term among Reformed theologians is "Total Inability."** Total Depravity names the corruption; Total Inability names the result. A man may be totally depraved and still be externally moral. What he cannot do is seek God, please God, or come to God apart from irresistible grace.

---

## PART TWO: THE BIBLICAL CASE

### 2.1 — The Fall and Its Consequences (Genesis 2–3)

The doctrine does not originate in systematic theology. It originates in the narrative of Scripture.

> **Genesis 2:17 (LSB)**
> *"But from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat, for in the day that you eat from it you will surely die."*

The penalty pronounced was death — not merely physical death, but spiritual death. Adam ate. The penalty was executed. What died in Adam was not his body alone but his standing before God, his spiritual orientation, and the spiritual life of every descendent in him.

> **Genesis 6:5 (LSB)**
> *"Then Yahweh saw that the wickedness of man was great on the earth, and that every intent of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually."*

Note the comprehensiveness of the language: *every intent* — *only evil* — *continually*. This is not a description of particularly wicked individuals. This is God's assessment of the human heart as a category.

> **Genesis 8:21 (LSB)**
> *"...for the intent of man's heart is evil from his youth..."*

This statement is made **after the flood**. The flood did not fix the problem. The corruption is not environmental. It is native to human nature after the Fall.

---

### 2.2 — The Psalms

> **Psalm 14:1-3 (LSB)**
> *"The fool has said in his heart, 'There is no God.' They are corrupt, they have committed detestable deeds; there is no one who does good. Yahweh has looked down from heaven upon the sons of man to see if there are any who understand, who seek after God. They have all turned aside, together they have become corrupt; there is no one who does good, not even one."*

Paul cites this passage directly in Romans 3. The universal language — *all turned aside*, *no one who does good*, *not even one* — is not hyperbole. It is a theological verdict on the human race apart from grace.

> **Psalm 51:5 (LSB)**
> *"Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin my mother conceived me."*

David does not locate the origin of his sin in his environment, his choices, or his circumstances. He locates it in his conception. This is the biblical basis for original sin as a native condition — not merely imitated behavior.

> **Psalm 58:3 (LSB)**
> *"The wicked are estranged from the womb; these who speak lies go astray from birth."*

Again, the language is natal. The corruption precedes moral choice. It is not learned — it is inherited.

---

### 2.3 — The Prophets

> **Jeremiah 17:9 (LSB)**
> *"The heart is more deceitful than all else and is desperately sick; who can understand it?"*

The Hebrew word translated *desperately sick* (*'anush*) carries connotations of incurable, mortally ill. This is not a minor ailment. It is terminal. And the prophet adds the rhetorical question: *who can understand it?* — the depraved heart is not merely corrupt but opaque to itself. Self-diagnosis is unreliable.

> **Isaiah 64:6 (LSB)**
> *"For all of us have become like one who is unclean, and all our righteous deeds are like a filthy garment; and all of us wither like a leaf, and our iniquities, like the wind, take us away."*

This text is striking because it does not say our *sinful* deeds are filthy — it says our *righteous* deeds are. Even the best acts of the unregenerate, when offered as merit before God, are as a filthy garment. This dismantles any basis for merit-based standing before God.

---

### 2.4 — The Apostle Paul

Paul's treatment of total depravity is the most systematic in Scripture.

> **Romans 3:10-12 (LSB)**
> *"as it is written, 'There is no righteous person, not even one; there is no one who understands, there is no one who seeks for God. All have turned aside, together they have become useless; there is no one who does good, there is not even one.'"*

This is a catena — a chain of Old Testament quotations assembled to produce a comprehensive indictment. Paul is not making an argument from isolated texts. He is demonstrating that the verdict on fallen humanity runs from Genesis through the Psalms through the Prophets as a single, unbroken testimony.

> **Romans 5:12 (LSB)**
> *"Therefore, just as through one man sin entered into the world, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men, because all sinned—"*

This is the Pauline foundation of original sin and federal headship. Death spread to *all* because *all sinned* — in Adam, their federal head. This is not mimetic (we sin because we watch Adam). This is federal and organic (we sin because we were in Adam).

> **Romans 8:7-8 (LSB)**
> *"because the mind set on the flesh is hostile toward God; for it does not subject itself to the law of God, for it is not even able to do so, and those who are in the flesh cannot please God."*

The key phrase: *it is not even able to do so.* This is not moral weakness. This is moral inability. The flesh-mind does not struggle to submit to God — it *cannot* submit to God. This is Total Inability stated with apostolic plainness.

> **1 Corinthians 2:14 (LSB)**
> *"But a natural man does not accept the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him; and he cannot understand them, because they are spiritually discerned."*

The natural man — the unregenerate man — does not merely *choose not to* receive spiritual truth. He *cannot* receive it. The incapacity is ontological, not merely volitional. It requires a new nature to perceive and receive spiritual reality.

> **Ephesians 2:1-3 (LSB)**
> *"And you were dead in your transgressions and sins, in which you formerly walked according to the course of this world, according to the prince of the power of the air, of the spirit that is now working in the sons of disobedience. Among them we too all formerly lived in the lusts of our flesh, doing the will of the flesh and of the mind, and were by nature children of wrath, even as the rest."*

This text contains four decisive claims about the unregenerate condition:

1. **Dead** — not sick, not weakened, not confused. Dead.
2. **Walking according to the course of this world** — the trajectory of the unregenerate life is worldward, not Godward.
3. **Under the dominion of the prince of the power of the air** — Satan has operative influence over the unregenerate. This is not external temptation only; it is internal directional control.
4. **By nature children of wrath** — the wrath of God is not a response to adult moral choices alone. It is the native condition of those born into Adam.

---

### 2.5 — The Words of Christ

> **John 6:44 (LSB)**
> *"No one can come to Me unless the Father who sent Me draws him; and I will raise him up on the last day."*

The Lord Jesus Christ Himself taught total inability. The word *can* (*dunamai*) is a word of capacity, not permission. The statement is not: "No one is allowed to come to Me unless the Father draws him." It is: "No one has the ability to come to Me unless the Father draws him." The inability is natural, not penal.

> **John 8:43-44 (LSB)**
> *"Why do you not understand what I am saying? It is because you cannot hear My word. You are of your father the devil, and you want to do the desires of your father..."*

Jesus does not say His audience *will not* hear. He says they *cannot* hear. And He explains it: they belong to their father the devil. Their inability is a function of their nature. They desire what their nature desires.

> **John 3:19-20 (LSB)**
> *"This is the judgment, that the Light has come into the world, and men loved the darkness rather than the Light, for their deeds were evil. For everyone who does evil hates the Light, and does not come to the Light so that his deeds will not be exposed."*

The diagnostic here is *love of darkness*. The unregenerate do not merely stumble in the dark — they love it. The problem is not ignorance that education can fix. It is affection. The will follows the affections, and the affections are set against God.

---

## PART THREE: CONFESSIONAL STANDARDS

### Westminster Confession of Faith, Chapter VI — Of the Fall of Man, of Sin, and of the Punishment Thereof

> **WCF 6.2:** *"By this sin they fell from their original righteousness and communion with God, and so became dead in sin, and wholly defiled in all the parts and faculties of soul and body."*

> **WCF 6.4:** *"From this original corruption, whereby we are utterly indisposed, disabled, and made opposite to all good, and wholly inclined to all evil, do proceed all actual transgressions."*

The phrase *utterly indisposed, disabled, and made opposite to all good* is a comprehensive statement of total depravity. The WCF does not say man is merely weakened or disadvantaged. It says he is *opposite to all good* — his orientation is fundamentally inverted.

---

### Heidelberg Catechism, Q&A 5, 8

> **Q. 5:** *"Can you keep all this perfectly?"*
> **A.** *"No, for I am by nature prone to hate God and my neighbor."*

The Catechism names the problem with pastoral directness: we are *by nature* prone to hate God. This is not a problem that arises from bad choices. It is what we are before any choices are made.

> **Q. 8:** *"But are we so corrupt that we are wholly incapable of any good and prone to all evil?"*
> **A.** *"Yes, unless we are regenerated by the Spirit of God."*

The conditional clause — *unless we are regenerated* — is everything. The solution to total depravity is not education, moral effort, or environmental improvement. It is regeneration by the Spirit of God.

---

### Canons of Dort, Third/Fourth Head of Doctrine

> **Article 3:** *"Therefore, all men are conceived in sin and are by nature children of wrath, incapable of any saving good, prone to evil, dead in sin, and in bondage to sin; and without the regenerating grace of the Holy Spirit, they are neither able nor willing to return to God, to reform the depravity of their nature, nor to dispose themselves to reformation."*

> **Article 4:** *"There is, to be sure, a certain light of nature remaining in man after the fall, by virtue of which he retains some notions about God, natural things, and the difference between what is moral and immoral, and demonstrates a certain eagerness for virtue and for good outward behavior. But this light of nature is far from enabling man to come to a saving knowledge of God and conversion to him — so far, in fact, that man does not use it rightly even in matters of nature and society."*

This is a masterful formulation. Dort acknowledges the light of nature (common grace), refuses to overstate it, and then precisely identifies its limits: it cannot bring a man to saving knowledge of God. It cannot even be used rightly in natural matters because sin distorts the use of every faculty.

---

### Belgic Confession, Article 15 — Original Sin

> *"We believe that by the disobedience of Adam original sin has been spread through the whole human race. It is a corruption of all nature — an inherited depravity which even infects small infants in their mother's womb, and the root which produces in man every sort of sin. It is therefore so vile and enormous in God's sight that it is enough to condemn the human race, and it is not abolished or wholly uprooted even by baptism..."*

The Belgic confession is notable for three things here: (1) it names the transmission as through *all nature*, not merely through individual choices; (2) it extends the corruption to *infants in the womb*, grounding the doctrine in Psalm 51:5; and (3) it explicitly rejects the notion that baptism abolishes original sin — a direct refutation of the Roman Catholic position.

---

## PART FOUR: THEOLOGICAL IMPLICATIONS

### 4.1 — Implication for Unconditional Election

If man is spiritually dead and unable to seek God, then election cannot be conditioned on foreseen faith. A dead man cannot produce the faith that would make him elect. The logic is sequential and necessary: Total Depravity demands Unconditional Election. If election were conditioned on foreseen faith, and faith is only possible through regeneration, and regeneration is only given to the elect — then the "foreseen faith" model collapses into a circle. God would be foreseeing what He Himself produces.

### 4.2 — Implication for Regeneration

Total Depravity requires that regeneration precede faith. This is one of the critical points at which Reformed theology distinguishes itself from all synergistic systems. In Arminian theology, the order is: *faith

# MORAL VS. NATURAL INABILITY
### A Reformed Study Guide | Iron & Ink Theological Series

---

## I. INTRODUCTION: WHY THIS DISTINCTION MATTERS

The distinction between **moral inability** and **natural inability** is not a theological abstraction. It is the precise point at which Reformed soteriology either stands or collapses under the weight of the sinner's accountability.

The objection is perennial: *If man cannot come to God — if he is truly bound by total depravity — then how can God hold him responsible for not coming?* The answer does not require softening the doctrine of total depravity. It requires understanding **what kind of inability** total depravity produces.

Get this distinction wrong and you will either:
1. Understate human inability (Arminianism), making grace cooperative rather than sovereign, or
2. Lose the ground for human accountability, conceding the libertarian premise that *ought implies can*.

The Reformed tradition refuses both errors.

---

## II. DEFINING THE TERMS

### Natural Inability
A **natural inability** is a deficiency in the **faculties themselves** — the person literally lacks the equipment required to perform the act.

> A blind man cannot read fine print. A deaf man cannot hear a symphony. A man without legs cannot walk.

Natural inability **removes culpability**. We do not hold a congenitally blind man guilty for failing to see. His inability is constitutive — it is a defect in his nature as a creature, not a defect in his will as a moral agent.

If man's inability to come to God were **natural** in this sense, the Arminian objection would be correct. A God who condemns men for failing to do what they literally have no faculty to do would be unjust.

---

### Moral Inability
A **moral inability** is a bondage of the **will itself** — not a missing faculty, but a **corrupted disposition**. The person possesses every natural faculty required for the act but is enslaved by a will that is wholly opposed to it.

> The unregenerate man has eyes, ears, intellect, will, and conscience. He hears the gospel with his ears. He processes its content with his mind. He *will not* come to Christ — not because his legs are broken, but because his heart is stone.

Moral inability **does not remove culpability**. The drunkard who cannot stop drinking has a real inability — his appetites master him — but we rightly hold him accountable because the bondage is self-generated through moral failure, not imposed from outside through a defect of nature.

---

## III. THE THEOLOGICAL ARCHITECTURE: WHERE THIS DOCTRINE LIVES

This distinction is the **load-bearing joint** between two doctrines that must both be held without compromise:

| Doctrine | Claim | Grounded In |
|---|---|---|
| Total Depravity | Man cannot and will not come to God | Moral inability — the will is enslaved to sin |
| Human Accountability | Man is justly condemned for not coming | The faculties are intact — only the disposition is corrupt |

These are not in tension. They are **mutually required**. Total depravity is not a mitigating factor at the judgment. It is the precise condition for which the sinner stands condemned.

---

## IV. SCRIPTURAL FOUNDATION

### A. The Inability Is Real and Thoroughgoing

**John 6:44 (LSB)**
> *"No one is able to come to Me unless the Father who sent Me draws him; and I will raise him up on the last day."*

The word translated "able" (δύναται, *dunatai*) is unqualified. There is no partial coming, no cooperative first step. The ability to come is entirely dependent on the Father's drawing.

**John 6:65 (LSB)**
> *"For this reason I have said to you, that no one is able to come to Me unless it has been granted him from the Father."*

Note the parallel: inability (v.65) and granting (v.65) are directly linked. The inability is overcome only by sovereign gift, not by the exercise of a natural faculty.

**Romans 8:7-8 (LSB)**
> *"...because the mind set on the flesh is hostile to God; for it does not subject itself to the law of God, for it is not even able to do so, and those who are in the flesh are not able to please God."*

The inability here is explicitly **moral** in character: *hostility* (ἔχθρα, *echthra*) is the root. The mind is not missing cognitive apparatus. It is **set against** God. The inability flows from the orientation of the will, not from the absence of faculties.

**1 Corinthians 2:14 (LSB)**
> *"But a natural man does not accept the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him; and he is not able to understand them, because they are spiritually appraised."*

Again: not a failure of intellect per se, but a failure of **spiritual reception**. The word "accept" (δέχεται, *dechetai*) carries volitional weight — it is a welcoming, a receiving. The natural man does not receive because he does not *want* to receive.

---

### B. The Accountability Is Equally Real

**John 3:19 (LSB)**
> *"This is the judgment, that the Light has come into the world, and men loved the darkness rather than the Light, for their deeds were evil."*

The condemnation is not *you could not come to the Light*. It is ***you loved the darkness***. The judicial sentence rests on moral preference, not natural deficiency.

**Romans 1:18-21 (LSB)**
> *"For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men who suppress the truth in unrighteousness, because that which is known about God is evident within them; for God made it evident to them... For even though they knew God, they did not honor Him as God or give thanks..."*

Suppression (κατεχόντων, *katechontōn*) is an active, willful act. Men do not fail to see God because the evidence is hidden. They actively *hold down* what they know. This is moral inability in its essence: full cognitive access, corrupted volitional response.

**John 5:40 (LSB)**
> *"...and you are unwilling to come to Me so that you may have life."*

Christ does not say *you are unable*. He says *you are unwilling*. The unwillingness **is** the inability. The will and the want have collapsed into the same enslaved disposition.

---

### C. The Distinction Assumed in the Prophets

**Jeremiah 13:23 (LSB)**
> *"Can the Ethiopian change his skin or the leopard his spots? Then you also can do good who are accustomed to doing evil."*

The analogy is precise. The Ethiopian's skin color is not a moral failing — it is simply the nature he has. But Jeremiah uses this as a parallel to moral bondage, which means the bondage is equally deep, equally constitutive of the person's present character — and yet is not a deficiency of natural faculties but a habituated moral condition.

---

## V. THE CONFESSIONAL ARTICULATION

### Westminster Confession of Faith — Chapter IX: Of Free Will

**WCF 9.3:**
> *"Man, by his fall into a state of sin, has wholly lost all ability of will to any spiritual good accompanying salvation; so as a natural man, being altogether averse from that good, and dead in sin, is not able, by his own strength, to convert himself, or to prepare himself thereunto."*

Note the precision: **averse** is a disposition of will. **Dead in sin** is a moral condition. Neither word describes a creature who lacks the natural equipment for moral agency. Both words describe a creature whose moral agency has been wholly corrupted in its orientation.

**WCF 9.1** (preceding):
> *"God hath endued the will of man with that natural liberty, that is neither forced, nor by any absolute necessity of nature determined to good or evil."*

The Westminster Divines did not collapse free will. They distinguished **natural liberty** (the will acts freely, without compulsion) from **moral bondage** (the will, acting freely, always chooses against God). The will is free from external coercion. It is enslaved to its own corrupt desires.

### Canons of Dort — Third/Fourth Head, Article 3:

> *"Therefore, all men are conceived in sin and are by nature children of wrath, unfit for any saving good, inclined to evil, dead in sins, and in bondage to sin; without the grace of the regenerating Holy Spirit, they are neither willing nor able to return to God, to reform the distorted tendencies of their nature, nor to dispose themselves to such reformation."*

**Neither willing nor able** — but note the order. The unwillingness precedes the inability in the logical structure. The inability flows from the will's corrupt orientation.

---

## VI. JONATHAN EDWARDS AND THE CLASSICAL FORMULATION

No theologian in the English-speaking Reformed tradition articulated this distinction with greater precision than **Jonathan Edwards** in *Freedom of the Will* (1754).

Edwards' framework:

> **Natural necessity** involves external compulsion or defect of natural faculty. A man who is literally chained cannot walk. This produces no moral guilt.

> **Moral necessity** involves the strongest disposition of the will determining its choices. The unregenerate man chooses sin with *moral necessity* — not because he is compelled from outside, but because his greatest love, his deepest want, his ruling desire is always and only for that which is not God.

Edwards' key insight: **the will always acts according to its strongest inclination**. For the unregenerate, the strongest inclination is always away from God. This is not compulsion. It is the free expression of a corrupt nature. The man does exactly what he wants to do — which is precisely his condemnation.

This allows Edwards — and Reformed theology broadly — to affirm:
- The will acts freely (no external coercion)
- The will is in bondage (its inclinations are wholly corrupted)
- The man is fully accountable (he does what he most wants to do)
- Grace must be monergistic (only regeneration can reorder the inclinations)

---

## VII. THE CRUCIAL IMPLICATION: "OUGHT IMPLIES CAN" REJECTED

Arminian theology — following the intuition of Kant and, before him, Pelagius — operates on the axiom: **if God commands it, man must have the natural ability to do it**. Otherwise the command is unjust.

The Reformed response is surgical:

1. **The command is just** because man is a moral agent with intact faculties — he has not been stripped of will, reason, or conscience.

2. **The inability is self-incurred** — not imposed arbitrarily by God, but generated through the fall. Adam acted as the federal head and covenant representative of humanity. The guilt and corruption that followed are justly ours.

3. **"Ought implies can" is a post-Kantian imposition** onto Scripture. The Bible consistently holds men responsible for sins they are constitutionally incapable of avoiding (apart from grace) without treating that responsibility as invalidated by the incapacity.

4. **The nature of the command reveals the nature of the need**. When God commands the dead to live (Ezekiel 37), he is not taunting them. He is acting sovereignly through the command. The command to repent and believe is simultaneously the instrument of sovereign grace in the elect and the just standard against which the non-elect are measured.

---

## VIII. COMMON OBJECTIONS AND REFORMED RESPONSES

### Objection 1: "If man cannot come, gospel preaching is pointless."

**Response:** This inverts the logic. Gospel preaching is not premised on the hearer's native ability to respond. It is the **instrument** through which the Spirit regenerates the elect (Romans 10:17; 1 Corinthians 1:18). The preacher's task is to proclaim faithfully. The Spirit's task is to give life. The distinction between moral and natural inability is precisely what makes preaching urgent rather than futile — the word goes out to those who have ears to hear (Matthew 11:15), knowing that God will grant hearing to whom He will.

---

### Objection 2: "Acts 17:30 says God commands all men everywhere to repent — this implies they can."

**Response:** The universality of the command establishes the universality of human *obligation*, not the universality of human *ability*. God commanding a rebellious creature to repent is not an implicit certification that the creature has the moral ability to do so. It is a just demand on a morally responsible agent. The inability is real — but it is **moral** inability, generated by the creature's own corrupt will, and therefore does not excuse.

---

### Objection 3: "Ezekiel 18:32 — 'I have no pleasure in the death of the one who dies, declares the Lord God. So turn and live.' This implies they can turn."

**Response:** The imperative does not establish the ability. "Turn" here reveals the **nature of the divine call** — it is a genuine, compassionate summons. It does not function as an anthropological statement about native human capacity. The text tells us what God desires and commands. It does not tell us that the spiritually dead have the moral power to generate life in themselves. Compare Ezekiel 37 — the command "live!" is issued to dry bones. The command does not imply the bones have the power to comply. The power comes with the command through the Spirit's sovereign action.

---

## IX. DIAGNOSTIC SELF-ASSESSMENT

Work through these questions with care. Do not move to the next until you have genuinely wrestled with the current one.

**Level 1 — Comprehension:**
1. Define natural inability in your own words. What makes it natural? What does it excuse?
2. Define moral inability in your own words. What makes it moral? Why does it not excuse?
3. What is the relationship between total depravity and moral inability? Are they the same thing or related concepts?

**Level 2 — Biblical Engagement:**
4. How does John 5:40 ("you are unwilling") function as a statement about inability? Why does Christ not say "you are unable"?
5. Walk through Romans 8:7-8. Identify every word or phrase that signals moral inability rather than natural inability.
6. How does Jeremiah 13:23 illuminate the distinction? What is the analogy doing theologically?

**Level 3 — Confessional Precision:**
7. How does WCF 9.3 preserve both the reality of the will's bondage and the justice of condemnation simultaneously?
8. Articulate Edwards' distinction between natural necessity and moral necessity. How does this framework answer the charge that Reformed theology makes God unjust?

**Level 4 — Polemical Readiness:**
9. A Pelagian says: "God would not command what man cannot do." Give a precise Reformed response that does not concede the premise.
10. An Arminian says: "The difference between moral and natural inability is just a semantic trick — inability is inability." How do you respond?
11. How does the fall of Adam factor into the justice of holding man accountable for a moral inability he did not personally choose?

---

## X. ADVERSARIAL DIALOGUE DRILL

*The following drill is designed for use in Dialogue Mode. You will argue the Reformed position. The AI will press the opposing objection at full strength. Do not begin until you are ready to defend the distinction without notes.*

**Opening Volley:**

You have just explained the moral/natural inability distinction to a well-read Arminian interlocutor. He listens carefully, then responds:

> *"You've drawn a careful distinction. I'll grant you that the will is disordered. But you've smuggled in something you haven't defended: the idea that a self-incurred inability is still a culpable inability. Consider a man who, through years of drug abuse, has destroyed his capacity for moral reasoning. We don't execute him for crimes committed in that diminished state — we recognize the addiction as a mitigating factor. Why should spiritual inability, even if self-generated through the fall, function any differently? You need a better account of why self-incurred inability still binds the conscience."*

**Engage this objection.**

---

## XI. FURTHER STUDY

### Primary Sources
- Jonathan Edwards, *Freedom of the Will* (1754) — Part I, Section 4; Part IV
- John Calvin, *Institutes of the Christian Religion* — Book II, Chapters 2–5
- Francis Turretin, *Institutes of Elenctic Theology* — Topic X, Questions 1–5

### Confessional Documents
- Westminster Confession of Faith, Chapter IX
- Canons of Dort, Third/Fourth Head, Articles 1–4
- Belgic Confession, Articles 14–15

### Modern Works
- R.C. Sproul
